Predicting the Future of Latin and Greek Plurals

Team Members: 김태오, 서병호, 엄찬우, 주지현

1. Introduction

Studying Latin and Greek plural forms in English is important because these ancient languages are the foundation of Western academic writing. Over 60% of English words come from Latin and Greek. Since many English words—especially in science, medicine, technology, and law—come from these roots, knowing their plural rules helps us communicate more clearly in professional jobs.

In this report, our team looks at which nouns are changing to regular English plurals and which ones still use their old forms. We want to explain why this happens by looking at how these words are used in different subjects and over long periods of time. Finally, we will use our data to predict which plural forms will become the standard by the year 2040.

2. Research Questions

  • Which nouns are shifting toward regularized English plurals (for example, -s or -es), and which retain their classical forms?
  • Why does this difference occur?
  • What patterns emerge when usage data is examined across registers, disciplines, and time periods?
  • Which plural forms are most likely to be treated as standard by 2040?

3. Corpus-Based Usage Analysis

This section presents long-term usage patterns for Latin-derived plural nouns based on corpus data from Ngram and COCA.

A. General Retention

In these cases, Latin-derived plural forms are consistently maintained, particularly in specialized or academic contexts where precision and tradition are valued.

Funguses / Fungi

Contextual Difference: Funguses: An English-style plural that is non-specialized and rarely used in everyday contexts. Fungi: A Latin-based plural used in biology and medicine to refer to microorganisms.

Looking at the Ngram graph, the English plural form “funguses” is hardly used throughout the entire period and maintains a very low frequency. In contrast, “fungi” begins to increase gradually from the late 19th century, shows a sharp rise after the mid-20th century, and continues to maintain a high frequency of usage up to the present.

According to COCA, “fungi” is used alongside words such as plants, species, and infection, and mainly appears in academic contexts such as biology and medicine. On the other hand, “funguses” appears extremely rarely, making it difficult to identify any clear contextual usage pattern.

Cactuses / Cacti

Contextual Difference: Cactuses: An English-style plural used to refer to cacti as plants or decorative houseplants in everyday contexts. Cacti: A Latin-based plural used in biology or botany when describing species classification or ecology.

Looking at the Ngram graph, the Latin plural “cacti” shows a steady increase beginning in the late 19th century, with a significant peak around the mid-20th century, and has maintained a dominant frequency compared to the English plural. In contrast, “cactuses” has remained at a consistently lower frequency throughout the entire period, though it shows a slight upward trend starting in the early 2000s.

According to COCA, “cacti” is frequently collocated with words such as species, yucca, native, and prickly, appearing primarily in scientific, botanical, or ecological contexts. On the other hand, “cactuses” appears alongside more general terms like you, I, and plants, indicating a stronger tendency toward everyday or conversational usage in genres such as news, magazines, and fiction.

Radiuses / Radii

Looking at the Ngram graph, the Latin plural “radii” maintains a consistently higher frequency across the entire period, with a noticeable increase around the mid-20th century and relatively stable usage afterward. In contrast, the English plural “radiuses” remains extremely rare throughout the entire period, showing very low frequency.

According to COCA, “radii” appears with words such as solar, earth, and circle, and is mainly used in mathematical and scientific contexts. On the other hand, “radiuses” appears very rarely, making it difficult to identify any clear contextual usage pattern.

Spectrums / Spectra

Looking at the Ngram graph, the Latin plural “spectra” increases sharply from the mid-20th century and maintains a relatively high frequency thereafter. In contrast, the English plural “spectrums” remains at a very low frequency throughout the entire period, with only limited usage.

According to COCA, “spectra” frequently collocates with terms such as absorption, Raman, stars, and galaxies, which shows its strong association with scientific discourse, especially in physics and astronomy. In contrast, “spectrums” appears more often with general or abstract expressions such as political and different, suggesting broader metaphorical or non-technical usage.

B. Non-Standard Forms

In these cases, English-style plural forms are virtually non-existent in common usage, as the Latin-derived forms are so deeply entrenched in their specific fields.

Phenomenons / Phenomena

Contextual Difference: Phenomenons: A non-standard English-style plural that is exceptionally rare and lacks a distinct contextual niche. Phenomena: The standard Latin-derived plural used consistently across both academic and general contexts.

According to the Ngram graph, the Latin-derived plural “phenomena” has maintained a consistently much higher frequency across the entire period. In contrast, the English-style plural “phenomenons” remains at or near zero, indicating that it has never gained acceptance as a viable alternative.

Data from COCA further reinforces this disparity, showing 8,342 total occurrences for “phenomena” compared to only 54 for “phenomenons”. Even in more casual media like blogs or the web, the Latin form remains the overwhelming choice.

Additionally, dictionary data supports this pattern. Major English dictionaries either do not recognize “phenomenons” as a standard plural form or label it as non-standard. This further confirms that “phenomena” is the only widely accepted plural form in both academic and general usage.

Larvas / Larvae

Contextual Difference: Larvae: The standard Latin-derived plural consistently used in scientific and biological contexts. Larvas: A non-standard English-style plural that is virtually non-existent.

According to the Ngram graph, the Latin-derived plural “larvae” shows a significant increase in frequency starting in the early 1900s, reaching a peak around the late 1970s before stabilizing. In contrast, the English-style plural “larvas” remains at a near-zero frequency throughout the entire period, showing no adoption as a modern alternative.

Data from COCA further emphasizes this exclusivity, with “larvae” recording 3,110 total hits compared to only 4 for “larvas”. Specifically, “larvae” is most frequent in Academic contexts and Magazines, whereas “larvas” appears so rarely that it lacks any functional contextual pattern.

Dictionary evidence shows that “larvas” is not recognized as a standard plural form, while “larvae” is consistently listed as the correct plural.

C. Everyday / Versatile Usage

Formulas / Formulae

Contextual Difference: Formulas: An English-style plural used in everyday and commercial contexts. Formulae: A Latin-based plural used primarily in mathematics and science.

Looking at the Ngram graph, the English plural “formulas” began to increase gradually from the late 19th century and has maintained a dominant lead, showing a particularly sharp increase into the 2000s. In contrast, the Latin plural “formulae” maintains a relatively lower frequency and tends to plateau after the late 20th century.

According to COCA, “formulas” is frequently used alongside words such as products, infant, herbal, and benefit, mainly appearing in commercial or everyday contexts. On the other hand, “formulae” is used with words like empirical, mathematical, and logical, primarily occurring in academic research to ensure technical precision.

D. Contextual Distinction

Antennas / Antennae

Looking into the Ngram graph, the English plural form “Antennas” began to be used frequently during the mid-1900s, presumably after the invention and development of electronic communication technology. On the other hand, the use of “Antennae” have been declining over the same time period.

Through the use of COCA, “Antennas” is mainly used to refer to the technology (e.g., rooftop antennas).

“Antennae” is mainly used to refer to a living form’s feelers (e.g., legs and antennae). In other words, after the development of electronical apparatus, the standard plural form “Antennas” became frequent to distinguish between the two.

Stadiums / Stadia

As observed in the Ngram Graph above, the use of Stadia gradually declined whereas Stadiums slowly increased in frequency.

COCA shows that “stadia” is now used relatively rarely as the plural of “stadium” in everyday English. Instead, it more often appears in classical or archaeological contexts referring to the ancient unit of distance or specialized historical contexts. This helps explain why “stadiums” has become the dominant plural for sports venues in modern usage.

E. Replacement: Modernization

Campuses / Campii

Looking at the Ngram graph, the English plural “campuses” shows a sharp increase from the mid-20th century and maintains a high frequency up to the present. In contrast, the Latin plural “campii” remains almost nonexistent.

According to COCA, “campuses” appears with words such as college, university, and students, and is widely used in educational and everyday contexts. In other words, since “campus” is frequently used in practical and everyday contexts, the regular English plural “campuses” has become the standard form.

4. Rate and Direction Comparison

Rate of Change

Based on the data, the rate at which plural forms change varies significantly across different nouns. Nouns such as “phenomena” and “larvae” show almost no change over time, as their English-style forms are rarely used and remain at near-zero frequency. In contrast, nouns like “formulas” and “stadiums” demonstrate a much faster shift toward English pluralization, with the English forms becoming increasingly dominant in modern usage. Meanwhile, words such as “cacti” and “fungi” show a slower rate of change. Although English alternatives like “cactuses” and “funguses” exist, the Latin forms continue to be used more frequently, especially in formal contexts.

Direction of Change

The direction of change also differs depending on how each noun is used. Some nouns maintain their Latin-derived plural forms across most contexts, while others shift toward English-style plurals as they become more common in everyday language. In addition, certain nouns develop a form of contextual differentiation. For example, both “antennas” and “antennae” are used, but in different contexts, allowing speakers to distinguish meaning more clearly. A similar pattern can be seen with “stadiums” and “stadia,” where the forms serve different communicative purposes.

Factors Explaining Variation

These differences can be explained by several key factors related to usage and context.

First, specialization plays an important role. Words such as “fungus” are primarily used in scientific and professional domains, which limits the spread of English plural forms. As a result, the Latin plural “fungi” has become the standard, and the two forms do not develop separate meanings but instead show a pattern where one form is used almost exclusively. A similar tendency can be observed with “cacti.” The Latin plural remains dominant due to its strong association with academic and scientific usage, while “cactuses” appears only occasionally in less formal contexts. In this case, the difference between the two forms is largely determined by usage context rather than meaning. This pattern is even more extreme in nouns such as “phenomena” and “larvae.” Their English counterparts (“phenomenons” and “larvas”) have not developed as standard forms, largely due to their limited usage environments and lack of dictionary recognition. Consequently, the Latin plurals remain the overwhelmingly dominant forms across all contexts.

Another important factor is the influence of everyday usability, as seen in nouns such as “campuses.” Certain plural forms shift from traditional Latin forms to modern English forms, allowing the nouns to be used more naturally in everyday contexts. According to the Ngram data, these shifts mostly occur around the 20th century, particularly for nouns frequently used in daily conversations and practical environments like education and general communication. As a result, the more regular and intuitive English-style plural forms tend to replace the Latin plurals, showing that plural form change is strongly driven by frequency of use in non-academic settings.

In contrast, nouns such as “formulas” follow a different pattern. As these words became more widely used in everyday, commercial, and industrial contexts, the more regular and intuitive English plural form became dominant. While “formulae” is still used in technical fields, “formulas” is now more common in general usage.

Finally, some nouns shift forms to avoid ambiguity and clarify meaning. For example, “stadium” originally used “stadia” as its plural form. However, since “stadia” was also used as the plural of the metric unit “stadion,” confusion arose. As a result, the English plural “stadiums” developed to distinguish between meanings more clearly.

5. 2040 Usage Projections

Based on the corpus data, most nouns are expected to shift toward regular English plural forms by 2040. However, a small number of nouns such as “phenomena,” “larvae,” and “fungi” will likely remain stable. These words are mainly used in scientific and academic contexts, so the Latin forms will continue to be the standard. In contrast, nouns like “formulas” will become more dominant, while “formulae” will be limited to specialized usage. This is because words used in everyday and commercial contexts tend to adopt simpler English plural forms. For nouns such as “antennas” and “antennae,” both forms will likely remain in use, but with clearer differences in meaning. A similar trend is expected for “stadiums” and “stadia,” although “stadiums” will likely dominate in general usage. Overall, except for a few specialized cases, English-style plural forms are expected to become the dominant standard.

6. Recommendations for Dictionaries and Education Policy

Dictionaries should clearly label which plural forms are standard, non-standard, or used in specific contexts. In education, English-style plurals should be taught as the default, while important Latin forms should be introduced with clear explanations of their usage.

7. Conclusion

This study examined how Latin-derived plural forms are used in modern English across different contexts. The analysis showed that plural usage varies depending on factors such as specialization, frequency, and context. Some nouns, such as “phenomena,” “larvae,” and “fungi,” remain stable and continue to use Latin-derived plural forms, especially in academic and scientific fields. In contrast, many other nouns are shifting toward regular English plural forms as they become more common in everyday usage. In some cases, both forms are used but serve different meanings or contexts, as seen in words like “antennas” and “antennae.” Overall, the findings suggest that English plural forms are gradually becoming more regularized, with English-style plurals becoming dominant in most cases, while Latin forms are retained in specialized contexts.

8. References

  • Google Ngram Viewer. https://books.google.com/ngrams
  • Corpus of Contemporary American English (COCA). https://www.english-corpora.org/coca/
  • Cambridge Dictionary. https://dictionary.cambridge.org/
